pages.title: Welcome to Password Basics
pages.subtitle: Learn how strong passwords help protect your accounts, data, and organization.

pages.title: Why passwords matter
pages.text:
Passwords are one of the first lines of defense for protecting systems and information.

If an attacker gains access to a password, they may be able to log in as a legitimate user and access sensitive systems, files, or data.

Many security incidents begin with a compromised password.

pages.title: What makes a strong password
pages.text:
Strong passwords are difficult for attackers to guess or crack. A strong password typically:
  • Is long, ideally 12 characters or more
  • Does not contain personal information such as names or birthdays
  • Is unique to a specific account
  • Is not reused across multiple systems
In many cases, length is more important than complexity alone.

pages.title: Passphrases
pages.text: A passphrase is a longer password made of multiple words. Example: BlueRiverCoffeeTrainWindow Passphrases are often easier to remember than short, random-looking passwords, while still being harder for attackers to guess because of their length.

pages.title: Real-world password habits
pages.text:
In reality, many people choose passwords that are easy to remember. Common examples include:
  • Using a pet's name or family member's name
  • Using birthdays or simple patterns
  • Reusing the same password across multiple websites
  • Writing passwords on sticky notes or storing them in plain text
These habits are understandable because remembering many complex passwords is difficult. However, attackers often try these common patterns first when attempting to access accounts.

pages.title: Making passwords easier to manage
pages.text:
Security experts recognize that remembering many strong passwords is difficult. Instead of relying on memory alone, safer approaches include:
  • Using a password manager to store unique passwords
  • Using long passphrases instead of short weak passwords
  • Enabling multi-factor authentication (MFA) when available
These tools help protect accounts even when human behavior makes password management challenging.

image.alt: Password managers and MFA improving security

pages.title: Password reuse risks
pages.text: Using the same password across multiple websites is dangerous. If one website is breached, attackers often try the same password on other services. This technique is called credential stuffing. A single reused password can put multiple accounts at risk.

pages.title: Password managers
pages.text:
A password manager securely stores passwords so users do not have to remember them all. Benefits include:
  • Generating strong passwords automatically
  • Storing unique passwords for every site
  • Reducing the need to reuse passwords
Using a trusted password manager is safer than reusing passwords or storing them in insecure places.

pages.title: Protecting your passwords
pages.text:
To protect your passwords:
  • Never share passwords with anyone
  • Do not send passwords through email or messaging apps
  • Use different passwords for different accounts
  • Enable multi-factor authentication when available
Good password habits reduce the chance of account compromise.

pages.title: If you think a password is compromised
pages.text:
If you believe your password may be exposed:
  1. Change the password immediately
  2. Update any other accounts using the same password
  3. Report the incident according to your organization’s process
Quick action helps reduce the risk of unauthorized access.

pages.subtitle: This short quiz will test your understanding of password security basics.
